questions.prompt: What distinction drives the choice between RAG and fine-tuning?
options: Knowledge (what the model needs to know now → RAG) versus behavior (how it should respond → fine-tuning)
Model size versus dataset size
Open-weight versus proprietary models
Latency versus throughput
questions.explanation: RAG supplies current, attributable knowledge at request time; fine-tuning sculpts how the model behaves — style, format, domain dialect. They solve different problems and compose well: retrieve the facts, fine-tune the behavior.

questions.prompt: Why is fine-tuning unreliable for injecting facts?
options: Facts are stored in the tokenizer, not the weights
Small datasets can't reliably overwrite pre-training, the weights go stale as facts change, and you lose attribution
Fine-tuning only affects the model's vocabulary
Providers prohibit factual fine-tuning
questions.explanation: A few thousand examples are a rounding error against trillions of pre-training tokens, so new facts stick inconsistently. And unlike RAG, baked-in facts can't be updated without retraining or cited back to a source.

questions.prompt: Where does fine-tuning genuinely shine? (Select all that apply.)
options: Enforcing style and strict output formats
Teaching domain-specific dialect and conventions
Getting frontier-quality results on a narrow task from a small, cheap, fast model
Keeping the model current with this week's product changes
questions.explanation: Style, format, dialect, and narrow-task distillation into small models are fine-tuning's home turf — behavior, not facts. Freshness is RAG's job; weights go stale the day training ends.

questions.prompt: What is the recommended escalation order, and what should exist before you fine-tune?
options: Fine-tune first for best quality, then add RAG
Prompt → RAG → fine-tune, with hundreds+ of quality examples and a pre-built eval set before tuning
RAG → fine-tune → prompt
Whichever is cheapest this quarter
questions.explanation: Prompting is cheapest to try and reverse, RAG fixes knowledge gaps, and fine-tuning comes last — undertaken only with substantial quality data and an eval set built first, since without one you can't even tell whether the tune helped.

questions.prompt: What is supervised fine-tuning (SFT), mechanically?
options: Reinforcement learning against human preferences
Next-token prediction training on your examples, with the loss computed on the assistant tokens
Editing individual neurons that store target behaviors
Prompt optimization via gradient-free search
questions.explanation: SFT is the same next-token objective as pre-training, run on your curated examples — typically masking the loss so only assistant-response tokens teach. Small learning rates and few epochs sculpt behavior without rebuilding capability.

questions.prompt: Why is full fine-tuning so memory-hungry?
options: It stores a copy of the training dataset on the GPU
Beyond the weights, gradients and optimizer states cost roughly 12–16 bytes per parameter in total
It requires the context window to be doubled
Tokenizers expand during training
questions.explanation: Training needs weights plus gradients plus optimizer states (e.g., Adam's moments) — roughly 12–16 bytes per parameter, so an 8B model wants on the order of 100GB+. That's the memory wall LoRA exists to break.

questions.prompt: How does LoRA avoid that cost?
options: It quantizes the gradients to 1-bit
It freezes the base model and learns low-rank update matrices — under 1% of parameters — producing a tiny, swappable adapter that can be merged at zero inference overhead
It trains only the tokenizer embeddings
It trains on a random 1% sample of the data
questions.explanation: LoRA learns (alpha/r)·B·A low-rank updates beside frozen weights. Only the adapters need gradients and optimizer state. Typical knobs: r of 8–32, alpha ≈ r or 2r, targeting all attention + MLP linear layers — and adapters merge into the base for deployment.

questions.prompt: Which statements about QLoRA and forgetting are true? (Select all that apply.)
options: QLoRA stores the frozen base in 4-bit NF4, fitting an 8B model's training in under 10 GB
QLoRA quality is near-identical to LoRA, at somewhat slower training speed
Catastrophic forgetting is mitigated by low epochs, small learning rates, and regression evals
QLoRA trains the base weights in 4-bit precision
questions.explanation: QLoRA quantizes the frozen base to 4-bit NF4 while adapters train in higher precision — the base is stored, not trained, in 4-bit. That puts 8B fine-tunes in free-Colab territory at near-LoRA quality. Forgetting remains real: keep epochs low and run regression evals (Lesson 5).

questions.prompt: Why does the lesson say 'the model becomes the dataset'?
options: The dataset is stored inside the model for retrieval
SFT faithfully learns every pattern in the data — including inconsistencies and errors — so data quality directly becomes model behavior
The model's parameter count must match the dataset size
Training replaces the model's vocabulary with the dataset's
questions.explanation: Fine-tuning imitates the training examples. Inconsistent formats produce inconsistent outputs; labeling errors become learned mistakes. That's why data work is half the project and 'read your data' is non-negotiable.

questions.prompt: For behavioral fine-tunes, what does the quality-vs-quantity evidence say?
options: More data always wins regardless of quality
Hundreds to low thousands of expert-grade examples typically beat large noisy sets
Fewer than ten examples is the optimum
Quality only matters above one million examples
questions.explanation: Behavior is learnable from surprisingly few examples if they're consistent and correct. A small, expert-grade, format-consistent dataset routinely outperforms a 100× larger scrape with noise — which would teach the noise too.

questions.prompt: Which hygiene steps are non-negotiable before training? (Select all that apply.)
options: Deduplication, both exact and fuzzy
Decontamination against your eval set
One consistent format matching production inference
Shuffling the eval set into training to maximize data volume
questions.explanation: Duplicates overweight their patterns, eval contamination makes every later measurement meaningless, and format mismatches between training and inference squander the tune. Mixing eval data into training is the cardinal sin — build and guard the eval set first.

questions.prompt: What's the right way to use synthetic data from a stronger teacher model?
options: Generate at maximum scale and train on all of it unfiltered
Generate at scale, then filter hard — programmatic checks, LLM-as-judge, human sampling — since filtering is where the value lives
Avoid synthetic data entirely; it never helps
Use synthetic data only for the eval set
questions.explanation: Teacher-generated data is the standard way to reach scale, and distillation can bake an expensive mega-prompt into a small cheap model. But raw generations include errors and repetition — quality comes from aggressive filtering, and keeping real data in the mix avoids distribution collapse.

questions.prompt: Why must the training system prompt match the inference system prompt?
options: The API rejects mismatched system prompts
The tuned behavior is conditioned on the training prompt — deploy with a different one and you trained behavior you'll never invoke
System prompts are merged into the adapter file
It only matters for models above 70B parameters
questions.explanation: SFT teaches 'given this context, respond like this.' If training examples carry one persona and production uses another, the learned association never fires. Format consistency between training and inference is the same principle (Lesson 3).

questions.prompt: Which two hyperparameters matter most in a LoRA fine-tune, per the walkthrough?
options: Batch size and dropout
Learning rate (~2e-4) and number of epochs (1–2)
Warmup ratio and scheduler type
Sequence length and gradient checkpointing
questions.explanation: Learning rate too high garbles output, too low changes nothing; epochs beyond 1–2 are the main memorization risk. LoRA r/alpha (16/32 starting points) come next; batch size mostly affects stability and is handled with gradient accumulation.

questions.prompt: During training, eval loss starts rising while train loss keeps falling. What does this mean?
options: Training is converging normally — continue
The model is memorizing the training set; stop at that point
The learning rate is too low
The eval set is too small to be meaningful
questions.explanation: Diverging curves are the classic overfitting signature: the model is fitting your specific examples rather than the generalizable behavior. Frequent eval steps exist precisely to catch this turn early — and even a healthy curve is only a proxy until behavioral evals pass.

questions.prompt: Which statements about adapters at inference time are true? (Select all that apply.)
options: The saved adapter is tens of megabytes, not the full model
Merging bakes the adapter into the weights for drop-in serving with vLLM or Ollama
One base model can host many adapters, enabling per-task or per-customer variants
Adapters must be retrained for every inference request
questions.explanation: LoRA's deployment story is its best feature: tiny swappable adapter files, merge-and-unload for zero-overhead serving, or multi-LoRA serving where one base hosts many variants. Training happens once; inference just applies the learned matrices.

questions.prompt: Why isn't a healthy eval-loss curve sufficient evidence to ship?
options: Loss curves are usually computed incorrectly
Loss is averaged next-token accuracy on your examples — it doesn't measure target-task behavior, and it can't reveal damage to general capability
Eval loss only matters for full fine-tuning, not LoRA
Shipping requires train loss to reach exactly zero
questions.explanation: A model can lower loss by nailing boilerplate while botching decisions that matter, and a tune can improve the target task while quietly degrading everything else. Behavioral evaluation on three axes — target task, regression, economics — is the verdict.

questions.prompt: What is the honest baseline a fine-tune must beat?
options: The base model with no system prompt
The base model with your best engineered prompt — the alternative you'd actually deploy
A random-answer baseline
Last year's fine-tune
questions.explanation: The cheaper alternative on the escalation ladder is more prompt engineering, so comparing against a lazy zero-shot strawman proves nothing. Pairwise judging against the best-prompt baseline — with sides randomized for position bias — answers the real question.

questions.prompt: What belongs in the regression suite for a fine-tuned model? (Select all that apply.)
options: General instruction-following cases unrelated to the target task
Reasoning problems
Format compliance for formats other than the one trained
Only the target-task eval set, run twice
questions.explanation: Catastrophic forgetting shows up off-target: a model tuned for three-sentence answers may answer everything that way or lose reasoning flexibility. The regression suite deliberately tests what you didn't train — plus safety behavior — comparing base vs tuned.

questions.prompt: After several disciplined iterations, the fine-tune still can't beat the prompted baseline. What should you conclude?
options: Keep iterating indefinitely — fine-tuning always wins eventually
Abandon: the experiment showed this task didn't need fine-tuning, which is a cheap and respectable thing to learn
Ship it anyway since the training cost is sunk
The eval set must be wrong
questions.explanation: Ship/iterate/abandon is an evidence-driven decision. Failing to beat the prompted baseline is a normal outcome that saves you the permanent operational drag of retraining cadences and serving infrastructure for no quality gain. Sunk training cost is not a shipping argument.
